participants.extracted: Families were eligible if they met low-income criteria (adjusted gross income no more than 150% of the federal poverty limit), had a child aged 3 to 8 years, and the child showed disruptive behavior in the clinical range on the Eyberg Child Behavior Inventory (ECBI) Severity or Intensity scales. Exclusion criteria were a child developmental or physical disability that would prevent use of HNC skills, caregiver current substance abuse/dependence, mood, or psychotic disorder, and family involvement with the Department of Social Services for abuse or neglect. Of 48 families who completed the clinic eligibility interview, 24 were ineligible and 2 declined, leaving 22 eligible families who were randomized; 3 initial families served as practice cases, and the remaining 19 families (TE-HNC n=9, HNC n=10) were included in analyses. Among families completing both pre- and post-assessments, caregivers averaged 37 years of age, 87% were female, and 80% worked at least part-time; 53% of the children were boys, with a mean age of 5.67 years.

interventions.extracted: Technology-Enhanced Helping the Noncompliant Child (TE-HNC) was a smartphone-enhanced version of the established Helping the Noncompliant Child (HNC) behavioral parent training program. Both groups received standard HNC, which includes an orientation session followed by two phases: Phase I, Differential Attention (“Child’s Game”), where caregivers learn to increase positive attention, reduce competing verbal behavior, and ignore minor inappropriate behavior; and Phase II, Compliance Training (“Parent’s Game”), where caregivers learn to give clear instructions and use a nonphysical time-out procedure for noncompliance. TE-HNC added smartphone-based supports intended to increase engagement and generalization rather than replace therapist-guided care. These enhancements included short skills videos modeling each HNC skill, daily smartphone surveys of practice and progress to guide feedback, mid-week video calls with therapists, weekly videotaped home practice for review and corrective feedback, and text reminders about practice and session attendance. The intervention aimed to improve caregiver engagement, increase home practice and coparent involvement, and ultimately reduce child disruptive behavior.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure several outcomes related to treatment engagement, skill generalization, child disruptive behavior, program costs, and consumer satisfaction. Primary process outcomes included engagement measures assessed during treatment: session attendance (whether the family attended scheduled weekly appointments), mid-week call availability (whether the family was available for scheduled mid-week calls), and caregiver-reported home practice/skill generalization (whether daily HNC skill practice was completed and whether assigned worksheets were completed). For the TE-HNC arm, additional within-group engagement measures were collected during treatment via smartphone daily surveys of skill practice and progress, weekly video recordings of home practice, and open-ended post-assessment questions about coparent involvement. The primary child outcome was disruptive behavior, measured with the Eyberg Child Behavior Inventory (ECBI), using the Intensity subscale and Problem subscale at pre-assessment and post-assessment (within two weeks after treatment termination). Economic and acceptability outcomes included program start-up costs and implementation costs, collected during the study using therapist and resource records, and consumer satisfaction, measured at post-assessment with the HNC Consumer Satisfaction Scale.
